courses.label: Evidence

courses.nlmSummary: Pleading, joinder, preclusion, and procedure architecture.
courses.nlmFocus: the core civil procedure doctrines, pleading and joinder frameworks, preclusion issues, and the procedural architecture shaping the reading

courses.nlmSummary: Constitutional rights, tests, and state-action limits.

courses.nlmSummary: Animal-status doctrine, regulation, and policy tension.

firac.basePrompt:
Please use the FIRAC briefing template for this case. Use bold headings, bullets for the procedural history and facts, and plain paragraphs everywhere else. Do not use horizontal rules. Leave one blank line between sections so the result pastes cleanly into Microsoft Word.

This brief is for my __COURSE_NAME__ class, so emphasize __COURSE_FOCUS__.

Stick to the template and rely only on the uploaded opinion or assigned reading. Do not use external sources. Do not embed citations or source callouts in the response. I already know where the material came from.

If the record is unclear on a detail, say so briefly rather than guessing.
firac.toolPrompt:
Please use the FIRAC briefing template for this case. Use bold headings, bullets for the procedural history and facts, and plain paragraphs everywhere else. Do not use horizontal rules. Leave one blank line between sections so the result pastes cleanly into Microsoft Word.

Stick to the template and rely only on the uploaded opinion or assigned reading. Do not use external sources unless the task is citation-based and you need the opinion text itself. Do not embed citations or source callouts in the response. I already know where the material came from.

If the record is unclear on a detail, say so briefly rather than guessing.
firac.systemPrompt: You are a precise law-school case briefing assistant. Create a full FIRAC brief using only the case text or reliable opinion text you are given. Do not invent facts, do not embed citations or source callouts, and return only the finished brief, ready to paste into Microsoft Word.

notebooklm.basePrompt:
You have the case opinions and the full reading assignment for this class. Prepare me for the big-picture doctrinal concepts as well as the nuances surfaced in footnotes, notes, and points of discussion.

Follow the reading in the exact textbook or casebook order, using the order the cases appear in the reading. Focus on only the major cases unless a note, footnote, or shorter excerpt is necessary to explain the doctrine clearly.

Emphasize __DOCTRINAL_FOCUS__ and recite the facts heavily when they matter to the doctrine. Assume I am a rising 3L using this for pre-class preparation, class discussion, and outline building, so keep it information-dense and do not spend time on basics a 3L already knows.

Avoid shallow list-brain. I want a start-to-finish synthesis that weaves together doctrine, facts, tensions between the cases, and the framing choices made by the reading.

generalFocus.label: General
generalFocus.summary: Default walkthrough in reading order, centered on the major cases.
generalFocus.focus: the major doctrinal developments that shape the reading as a whole

styles.label: General MyVoice
styles.summary: Casual, smart DRQ answers that sound like you across classes.
styles.prompt:
Attached is the full reading for class today. I need short answers to the directed reading questions below. Answer every question posed. When appropriate, reference the Federal Rules of Evidence and any other rules, cases, or class materials that matter.

Match my voice: casual, direct, and smart, like a capable law student talking through the material without sounding robotic or overly formal. Aim for about 1 to 4 sentences per answer unless a question clearly needs more.

Put the full text of each question in bold before its answer. Do not embed citations. If you mention a rule, work it naturally into the sentence instead of sounding like a treatise.

styles.label: Civ Pro II Cold Call
styles.summary: Verbatim questions, quick-scan answers, and dense cold-call prep for Civ Pro II.
styles.prompt:
While including all of the original questions verbatim, please insert answers in the context of the reading and the cases we covered today. This is my Civil Procedure II class, I am an early 3L, and the answers should be quick to scan and data-dense rather than multiple paragraphs.

Imagine yourself having to read this and then answer a cold call without just reading a whole bunch of words out loud. I am fine with anywhere from 1 to 4 sentences per answer.

Put each original question in bold exactly as written before its answer. Ground the answers in the assigned reading and the cases from today. Keep the tone smart, direct, and classroom-usable. Do not embed citations unless I specifically ask for them.

justiceDetail.prompt:
I want you to prepare about 1 to 3 paragraphs for each justice in our reading for ONLY the 44 Liquormart case. In total, aim for about 1 page of text, and go to 2 pages only if absolutely necessary.

Use heavy highlighting through bolding and italics on the key colloquy, framing language, and doctrinal moves for each justice. Keep the writing tight, readable, and class-useful rather than bloated.

Stay strictly confined to 44 Liquormart. Do not bring in other cases from the reading except where a justice directly invokes them in a way that is necessary to explain that justice's reasoning. Focus on what each justice is doing, emphasizing the distinctive tone, constitutional framing, agreement or disagreement, and the most important lines of analysis.

evidenceExpert.prompt:
Act as a legal expert and analyze the following problem. Provide a concise, professionally written response that meets the following criteria:

1. Complete Analysis: Ensure that every single question or sub-issue posed in the problem is directly and thoroughly answered.
2. Authority and Citations: Explicitly identify and cite the legal authorities informing your analysis. You must point to the specific Federal Rules of Evidence (FRE), relevant Advisory Committee Notes (ACN), applicable case law, and general textbook principles that apply to this scenario.

Here is the problem/reading:
> [Insert your specific problem or reading text here]
